event: Saint Christopher with scenes from his life
symbols: Christ Child seated above — the burden of carrying Christ, the meaning of Christopher's name (Christ-bearer)
staff in hand — the pilgrim's journey and legendary river crossing
red garments and military attire — martyrdom and the soldier-saint tradition
gold background — divine glory and heavenly realm in Orthodox iconography
border scenes depicting life episodes — hagiographic narrative cycle of the saint's deeds and martyrdom
description: A large Orthodox icon with a central full-length figure of Saint Christopher standing upright, wearing red and military-style garments, holding a staff in one hand. Above him in the upper register sits a small enthroned figure of Christ. The central panel is surrounded by smaller border scenes depicting episodes from the saint's life, including encounters with rulers, miracles, and martyrdom. The background is gold leaf with Slavonic inscriptions across the top. The overall composition follows the traditional vita icon format.
theological_description: The vita icon of Saint Christopher presents the theology of Christian witness through its hagiographic narrative structure. Christopher, whose name means 'Christ-bearer,' embodies the call to carry Christ into the world, a vocation shared by all the baptized. The legendary account of his bearing the Christ Child across a river serves as an allegory for the weight of faith — the child grows heavier as the crossing proceeds, revealing that to carry Christ is to carry the sins of the world. The border scenes narrating his martyrdom reflect the apostolic teaching that faithful discipleship leads through suffering to glory (ROM.8.17). The presence of the enthroned Christ above Christopher illustrates the promise of Matthew 10:32 — that whoever confesses Christ before men will be acknowledged before the Father. The gold background signifies the uncreated light of God's glory, the eschatological reality into which the saints have entered.

scripture_refs.reason: Christopher's public confession and martyrdom for Christ echoes the promise of acknowledgment before the Father
Suffering with Christ to be glorified with him, illustrated by the saint's martyrdom scenes
The Christ-bearer theme — it is no longer I who live but Christ who lives in me
Be faithful unto death and I will give you the crown of life — the martyr's reward
